text: A 72-year-old man with a 10-day history of fever, nausea and vomiting, and progressive confusion remains hospitalized because his symptoms have not resolved despite treatment with broad-spectrum antibiotics. He has a 6-year history of severe emphysema treated with courses of corticosteroids during the past year. Medications include intravenous ceftriaxone and ampicillin, albuterol inhalers, theophylline, and prednisone. He is confused and stuporous. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 142/86 mm Hg. Physical examination shows meningismus. An MRI of the brain shows diffuse meningeal enhancement with a basilar predominance. A lumbar puncture is done; cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows a leukocyte count of 322/mm³ (86% lymphocytes), a glucose concentration of 17 mg/dL, and a protein concentration of 212 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct: Chronic use of immunosuppressants including glucocorticoids increase the likelihood of tuberculosis reactivation. Tuberculous meningitis typically presents with nuchal rigidity, fever, and altered mental status, similar to the symptoms seen in bacterial meningitis. However, unlike bacterial meningitis which presents acutely, tuberculous meningitis typically shows a subacute presentation. Presence of cranial nerve palsies is also more likely in tuberculous meningitis than bacterial meningitis. Physical examination may show a positive Kernig or Brudzinski sign, indicative of meningeal irritation. Results of laboratory studies will show nonspecific changes including leukocytosis and mild anemia. A tuberculin skin test or interferon-gamma release assay are usually positive, but a negative result is also possible. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) evaluation is the initial diagnostic step and will reveal an increased opening pressure, increased white blood cell count with prominent lymphocytes, increased protein concentration, and decreased glucose concentration. Culture of the CSF for acid-fast bacilli may be positive, though the sensitivity of this test for tuberculous meningitis is less than 50%. Radiologic evaluation may show basilar exudates, as in this case, as well as periventricular infarcts and hydrocephalus.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
Herpes simplex encephalitis (Choice A) presents with fever, altered mental status, and neurologic abnormalities, such as seizure, aphasia, hemiparesis, or cranial nerve deficits. Neuroimaging will typically show changes in the temporal lobe. The basilar predominance in this case is more suggestive of a tuberculous meningitis than herpes encephalitis.
Lyme disease (Choice B) caused by disseminated Borrelia burgdorferi infection may cause neuropathy and meningitis. However, this would be expected to be accompanied by a rash in the pattern of erythema migrans and history of tick exposure.
Neurosarcoidosis (Choice C) is defined as the presence of neurologic symptoms in a patient with known sarcoidosis. As any portion of the central or peripheral nervous system may be affected, symptoms may stem from cranial nerve palsies, hypothalamic dysfunction, spinal cord involvement, or peripheral neuropathy. This patient does not show other features of sarcoidosis, including pulmonary, eye, or skin involvement.
Neurosyphilis (Choice D) presents with fever, meningismus, ataxia, Argyll Robertson pupil, and tabes dorsalis. Tabes dorsalis refers to infection of the posterior columns and dorsal roots of the spinal cord causing impaired sensation of light touch, vibration, and proprioception along with ataxia. Serologic testing of the CSF for syphilis using a venereal disease research laboratory (VDRL) or rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test is positive.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Tuberculous meningitis typically presents in a subacute fashion with nuchal rigidity, fever, and altered mental status. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) evaluation is the initial diagnostic step and will disclose an increased opening pressure, increased white blood cell count with prominent lymphocytes, increased protein concentration, decreased glucose concentration, and positive culture for acid-fast bacilli.

text: An 8-month-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because of increased irritability over the past month. During this period, she has had intermittent low-grade fever and episodes of stiffening of her body. Her father reports that she previously interacted with them and smiled when prompted but has not made eye contact for the past 3 days. She has a history of hypotonia. She cannot yet roll over or sit up. Her parents are third cousins. She is awake and irritable and has poor visual regard. Examination of the skin shows no neurocutaneous lesions. The optic nerves are pale. Neurologic examination shows hyperextension of the head and back, ankle spasticity, and fisting. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk, and Babinski sign is present. An MRI of the brain is most likely to show which of the following?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

This infant's presentation of fever, spasticity, hypotonia, vision loss, optic nerve pallor, and Babinski sign, as well as a family history of consanguinity are consistent with a diagnosis of Krabbe disease, also known as galactosylceramide lipidosis. Krabbe disease is an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GALC gene that encodes galactocerebrosidase, the deficiency of which results in accumulation of galactocerebroside and demyelinating neuronal degeneration. Patients typically present within the first year of life with progressive neurologic degeneration, spasticity, stiffness, seizures, feeding difficulty, and peripheral neuropathy, including optic neuropathy. The diagnosis is confirmed with serum enzyme assays, although MRI of the brain will show white matter degeneration caused by demyelination. There is no effective treatment for Krabbe disease and most affected children die during the first several years of life.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, E, F, and G.
Cerebellar hypoplasia (Choice A) and hydrocephalus (Choice D) are both observed in Dandy-Walker malformation. Patients present during the first year of life with hydrocephalus and associated nausea, vomiting, seizures, and downward displacement of the eyes. Dandy-Walker malformation does not typically present with spasticity and stiffness and presents with papilledema rather than optic nerve pallor.
Holoprosencephaly (Choice C) is a congenital malformation in which the prosencephalon does not divide into two hemispheres. Most cases result in death in utero and are also accompanied by craniofacial malformations including cyclopia and the presence of a proboscis. Lysosomal storage disorders, including Krabbe disease, are not frequent causes of holoprosencephaly.
Periventricular calcifications (Choice E) is a frequent finding in several congenital infections, such as in congenital toxoplasmosis or cytomegalovirus infection. While such infections can have profound developmental consequences, they do not typically cause progressive neurodegeneration.
Schizencephaly (Choice F) is a rare congenital malformation characterized by clefts through the cerebral parenchyma between the ventricles and pia mater. Lysosomal storage disorders, including Krabbe disease, are not a frequent cause of schizencephaly.
No abnormalities (Choice G) would not be an expected finding in this infant with profound neurologic degeneration during the first year of life.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Krabbe disease is an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GALC gene that encodes galactocerebrosidase, the deficiency of which results in the accumulation of galactocerebroside and secondary demyelinating neuronal degeneration. Patients typically present within the first year of life with progressive neurologic degeneration, spasticity, stiffness, seizures, feeding difficulty, and peripheral neuropathy, including optic neuropathy.

text: A 57-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after a simple partial seizure. Examination shows mild weakness of the right lower extremity. A CT scan of the head shows a 1.5-cm, uniformly and intensely enhancing parasagittal mass in the left frontal region. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

Meningioma is a common, benign central nervous system (CNS) tumor derived from the meninges that occurs along the surfaces of the brain and spinal cord, often in the parasagittal region. They occur in women more often than men and are associated with neurofibromatosis type 2 and multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1. Patients may be asymptomatic or experience seizures or focal neurologic deficits related to the tumor location. Meningiomas are typically benign but can be malignant. Malignant meningiomas may grow rapidly, leading to symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (eg, postural headache, nausea, papilledema), or may metastasize. Meningiomas appear as well-circumscribed, extra-axial (ie, outside of the brain parenchyma) masses that compress the parenchyma. They show uniform enhancement on imaging. Definitive diagnosis requires histologic examination of tissue, which will show spindle cells arranged in a whorled pattern with psammoma bodies. Treatment may include antiepileptics or surgical resection to alleviate the tumoral mass effect if significant, though often, periodic surveillance is all that is necessary.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Astrocytoma (Choice A) is a primary CNS neoplasm that can occur as benign pilocytic astrocytoma in children or malignant glioblastoma multiforme (Choice C) in adults. Glioblastoma are highly malignant tumors appearing as intra-axial masses in the cerebral hemispheres, which typically show areas of necrosis and can spread to the opposite hemisphere across the corpus callosum.
Ependymoma (Choice B) is a CNS neoplasm formed from ependymal cells. Location typically involves the fourth ventricle. Histologic characteristics include perivascular pseudorosettes formed by malignant cells arranged around a blood vessel.
Schwannoma (Choice E) are benign tumors that arise from Schwann cells in peripheral nerve sheaths. They commonly affect the facial and vestibulocochlear nerves, and mass effect may result in sensorineural hearing loss or ataxia. They are associated with neurofibromatosis type 2.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Meningiomas are benign CNS tumors arising from the meninges that may compress the brain or spinal cord and cause focal neurologic deficits as well as seizures. Neuroimaging shows a spherical and well-circumscribed mass that indents the parenchyma. Lesions show uniform enhancement on imaging.

text: A 67-year-old semiretired corporate executive comes to the physician with his wife because she is concerned about his increasing disorganization at home over the past year. She also notes that he is unable to remember the names of recent acquaintances. He has insisted on working part-time since stepping down from a CEO position. His secretary has told his wife that he has been forgetting scheduled appointments and becomes angry and tearful when he is reminded. He drinks one to two cocktails each evening. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he is mildly agitated and has word-finding difficulty and short-term memory deficits. He is oriented to person, place, and time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Alzheimer dementia (AD) is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that begins with short-term memory impairment and decline in visuospatial abilities and progresses to apraxia, language abnormalities, and dysexecutive syndrome (resulting in the inability to recall scheduled appointments in this patient). The disease culminates in behavioral and personality changes that prevent the patient from performing basic activities of daily living such as bathing. AD may be treated with drugs that both directly and indirectly increase acetylcholine in synaptic clefts. These include donepezil and cholinesterase inhibitors which non-competitively and reversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase and thereby increase the amount of synaptic acetylcholine available for neurotransmission. Though these drugs may slow the rate of cognitive decline and modestly improve functionality, they are not curative for AD.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M, N, O, P, and Q.
Acute stress disorder (Choice A) refers to symptoms of intrusive thoughts, hyperarousal, dissociation, avoidance, and/or depressed mood within 1 month of a traumatic event.
Alcohol-induced amnestic disorder (Choice B) may be seen in alcoholics who are deficient in vitamin B₁ (thiamine). Patients often confabulate to compensate for impaired memory.
Dissociative amnesia (Choice D) is a potentially reversible impairment of memory regarding one's identity, usually in association with a history of psychological trauma.
General paresis (Choice E) is a finding of late stage tertiary syphilis in which dementia is accompanied by general paralysis.
Head trauma (Choice F) may cause a mild traumatic brain injury, also known as concussion, commonly resulting in loss of consciousness, confusion, amnesia, headache, dizziness, nausea, and/or vomiting minutes after the injury.
Hepatolenticular degeneration (Wilson disease) (Choice G) results from impaired metabolism of copper and causes neuropsychologic impairment including parkinsonism, ataxia, dementia, or hallucinations, in addition to liver disease.
HIV encephalitis (Choice H) is characterized by concentration impairment, depressive symptoms, and cognitive and motor retardation seen in patients with poorly controlled HIV.
Huntington disease (Choice I) presents with insidious onset of psychiatric and cognitive symptoms, followed by motor symptoms (chorea, hypotonia, dystonia, loss of voluntary control of movements).
Hypercalcemia (Choice J) may present with anxiety, depression, and/or cognitive dysfunction but not progressive memory loss.
Major depressive disorder (Choice K) may cause cognitive impairment in older patients but would also disclose depressed mood, anhedonia, or other signs of a depressive disorder.
Multi-infarct (vascular) dementia (Choice L) features stepwise cognitive decline following cerebrovascular accidents.
Niacin deficiency (Choice M) leads to pellagra, characterized by diarrhea, dementia, and dermatitis.
Normal-pressure hydrocephalus (Choice N) presents with symptoms of dementia, urinary incontinence, and ataxia.
Parkinson disease (Choice O) is characterized by tremor, rigidity, akinesia, and postural instability.
Pick disease (Choice P), or frontotemporal dementia, is a neurodegenerative disorder characterized by atrophy of the frontal and temporal lobes, causing deficits in social behavior or personality along with dementia.
Normal aging (Choice Q) includes a slow decline of executive functioning, attention span, and problem-solving after the age of 70, but a successfully aging individual should be able to still function in society.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Alzheimer dementia is the most prevalent dementia, presenting with progressive cognitive decline that culminates in the inability to perform basic activities of daily living.

text: A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 2 hours after he dove into a shallow lake and struck the back of his head on the bottom. He immediately had the onset of pain in his neck and a sensation of an electric shock radiating from his neck to his left shoulder. He did not have loss of consciousness. He was able to stand, and two friends helped him out of the water into a supine position on the dock. At the scene, his spine was immobilized with a cervical collar, and his head was taped to a backboard. On arrival, he is alert. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 3/5 on extension of the left wrist. Deep tendon reflexes are normal. Babinski sign is absent. Sensorimotor examination of the right upper extremity and lower extremities shows no abnormalities. Sensation to light touch is decreased over the radial aspect of the left forearm and hand and over the palmar and dorsal aspects of the left thumb. Rectal examination shows normal sphincter tone and sensation. A lateral x-ray of the cervical spine is shown. This patient most likely has which of the following cervical spine injuries?

options.B: Bilateral dislocation of facets with impingement of the left C5 nerve root
options.C: Bilateral dislocation of facets with impingement of the left C6 nerve root
options.E: Unilateral dislocation of a facet with impingement of the left C5 nerve

explanation.correct: This patient likely has bilateral dislocation of facets with impingement of the left C6 nerve root caused by cervical spinal injury. The C6 dermatome is located on the lateral (radial) aspect of the forearm and extends to the lateral two digits of the hand. Injury to the C6 nerve root will cause decreased sensation, pain, and/or paresthesia in these distributions (or part of this distribution) along with motor dysfunction of elbow flexion and wrist extension. The C6 nerve root carries efferent information for the biceps reflex, so patients with C6 nerve injury may present with a diminished or absent biceps reflex. Dermatomes and myotomes are components of the embryonic somite, a paired segment of paraxial mesoderm. When fully developed, the dermatome is an area of skin whose afferent fibers travel within a single spinal nerve, and myotome is a group of muscles innervated by efferent fibers from a single spinal nerve. Disc herniation and spondylosis are common causes of spinal nerve root compression in older patients, and trauma and consequent vertebral dislocation or fracture is a common cause of root compression in younger patients such as in this case. Sensory deficits, motor dysfunction, and pain typically occur in the dermatomal and myotomal distribution that correlates to the affected nerve root.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Anterior spinal artery thrombosis (Choice A) typically manifests with bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation and motor function below the level of the lesion, along with autonomic dysfunction. The anterior spinal artery supplies the corticospinal, spinothalamic, and autonomic tracts.
Bilateral dislocation of facets with impingement of the left C5 nerve root (Choice B) and unilateral dislocation of a facet with impingement of the left C5 nerve (Choice E) would lead to sensory and motor dysfunction in the lateral aspect of the shoulder and upper arm. C5 nerve compression typically results in an absent biceps reflex, as the C5 nerve root carries efferent information for this reflex. However, C5 nerve compression would not cause decreased sensation in the digits.
Brown-Séquard syndrome (Choice D) refers to hemisection of the spinal cord, which damages the anterior horn along with three major spinal cord tracts: the corticospinal tract, dorsal column-medial lemniscus tract, and spinothalamic tract. Because of the decussation of the spinothalamic tract inferior to the lesion, patients typically present with contralateral impairment in pain and temperature sensation and ipsilateral deficits in other sensory domains and motor function.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Cervical spinal injury can lead to vertebral dislocation and impingement of the spinal nerve root, which leads to weakness, numbness, paresthesia, and pain in the dermatomal and myotomal distribution that correlates to the affected root. C6 nerve root compression will cause decreased sensation, pain, and/or paresthesia in the lateral forearm and lateral two digits. Additionally, C6 root compression causes motor dysfunction of elbow flexion and wrist extension.

text: A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after the sudden onset of headache and left-sided weakness that have become increasingly severe. He has hypertension treated with lisinopril and chronic neck pain treated with 325-mg aspirin. On arrival, he appears mildly lethargic. His pulse is 60/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 180/100 mm Hg. Examination shows moderate weakness of the lower left side of the face. Muscle tone is decreased in the left upper and lower extremities. Muscle strength is 4/5 on the left and 5/5 on the right. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ on the left. Babinski sign is present on the left. A CT scan of the head without contrast is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

Hypertensive intracerebral hematomas (ICHs) can occur in patients with chronic hypertension, typically because of hemorrhage from small penetrating arteries in the pons, midbrain, thalamus, putamen, caudate, or cerebellum. Risk factors for hypertensive ICH include uncontrolled hypertension and antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy, such as with aspirin or warfarin. Patients can present with headache, vomiting, confusion, altered mental status, stupor, or coma. Focal neurologic deficits depend on the site of the ICH and can include hemiparesis, hemisensory symptoms, gaze palsy, dysarthria, aphasia, or if in the cerebellum, ataxia, vomiting, and disequilibrium. Patients may manifest upper motoneuron examination findings such as spasticity, hyperreflexia, and extensor plantar reflex (Babinski sign). Diagnosis is made with noncontrast CT scan of the head which will show hyperdense intraparenchymal products, which is the appearance of acute bleeding in the brain as seen in this patient's right hemisphere.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (Choice A) occurs secondary to amyloid deposition in cerebral blood vessels and leptomeninges. It can cause spontaneous intracerebral hemorrhage in older adults, typically in a lobar location, because of the location of amyloid deposits in cortical vessels. In contrast, hypertensive hemorrhages typically occur in a deep location such as the putamen, thalamus, midbrain, or pons.
Ischemic cerebral infarction (Choice C) can occur as a result of thromboembolic occlusion of cerebral arteries. Patients typically present with acute focal neurologic deficits, especially on one side of the body, and may have speech difficulty, dysarthria, or aphasia. A CT scan would not show hyperdense blood products unless the infarction is complicated by hemorrhagic conversion.
Ruptured arteriovenous malformation (Choice D) can lead to intracerebral or subarachnoid hemorrhage. Arteriovenous malformations are uncommon and usually present in individuals younger than 50 years old with intracranial hemorrhage, seizure, neurologic deficit, or headache, or may be an incidental finding. In an older patient with uncontrolled hypertension, a hypertensive intracerebral hematoma is more likely.
Ruptured berry aneurysm (Choice E) typically leads to a subarachnoid hemorrhage. Patients typically present with an acute, severe headache (often described as the worst headache of the patient's life), along with neck stiffness, photophobia, and possible stupor or coma in severe cases. CT scan of the head typically shows hyperdense blood products in the cerebral sulci and basal cisterns.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Hypertensive intracerebral hematomas can occur in patients with chronic hypertension, typically as a result of hemorrhage from small penetrating arteries in the pons, midbrain, thalamus, putamen, caudate, or cerebellum. Patients can present with headache, vomiting, confusion, altered mental status, stupor, upper motoneuron signs, and focal neurologic deficits such as hemiparesis and hemisensory symptoms.

text: Over the past 6 months, an otherwise healthy 19-year-old cheerleader has had increasingly severe neurologic symptoms. Initially, she had difficulty jumping; 2 weeks later she had a tingling feeling in the toes of her right foot that quickly spread to the left foot. Over the past 2 weeks, she has stumbled and fallen several times. Examination of the upper extremities shows normal findings. Deep tendon reflexes are absent in the lower extremities. She is unable to heel walk and has mild difficulty walking on her toes and rising from a low squatting position. Position sensation is normal, but pinprick feels less sharp distally and occasional light touch stimuli are missed over the toes. Peroneal motor conduction velocity is moderately slowed (22 m/sec; N=42–57). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy (CIDP) is an immune-mediated demyelinating condition affecting the spinal nerve roots. The pathogenesis of CIDP involves inflammation of myelin by an unknown mechanism. The ventral nerve roots containing motor efferents are commonly involved, though the dorsal nerve root (controlling sensation) may also be affected. As such, patients typically present with relapsing-remitting or gradually progressive symmetric muscle weakness with depressed or absent deep tendon reflexes (reflecting a lower motoneuron pattern of dysfunction) that affects the proximal and distal lower extremities simultaneously and may also affect the upper extremities. Autonomic dysfunction may occur but is typically mild. Sensory deficits may occur (though these symptoms are typically mild compared to motor dysfunction). A slowing of conduction velocity on nerve conduction studies indicates demyelination and supports the diagnosis. The diagnosis is further supported by increased cerebrospinal fluid protein with normal cell counts on lumbar puncture, termed albuminocytologic dissociation and thought to reflect generalized inflammation. Management includes glucocorticoids, immunomodulators, plasma exchange, or intravenous immunoglobulin therapy.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, and L.
Diabetic neuropathy (Choice B) commonly results in sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy. This patient has no history of diabetes mellitus.
Epidural spinal cord metastasis (Choice C) and spinal epidural abscess (Choice H) may result in spinal cord compression, which typically leads to sensory, motor, and/or autonomic dysfunction below the level of the lesion in an upper motoneuron pattern of weakness.
Guillain-Barré syndrome (Choice D) is an inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy that presents with acute-onset, ascending, bilateral muscle weakness with hyporeflexia. A chronic time course is inconsistent with Guillain-Barré syndrome.
Multiple sclerosis (Choice E) is an inflammatory demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. If present, motor dysfunction would present in an upper motoneuron pattern.
Spinal cord infarction (Choice F) typically occurs in the territory of the anterior spinal artery, which usually manifests with loss of pain and temperature and upper motoneuron dysfunction below the level of the lesion, along with autonomic dysfunction.
Primary spinal cord tumors (Choice G) affecting the cauda equina can lead to unilateral or bilateral lower extremity radicular pain and sensorimotor dysfunction. Pain is generally present.
Spondylotic myelopathy (Choice I) refers to degenerative vertebral disease leading to spinal cord damage. This otherwise healthy young patient is unlikely to show vertebral degenerative disease.
Syringomyelia (Choice J) features a cystic cavity in the central spinal cord and manifests with deficits in pain and temperature sensation and lower motoneuron dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities.
Vasculitic neuropathy (Choice K) typically presents with painful polyneuropathy. This patient did not endorse pain.
Vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) deficiency (Choice L) can lead to subacute combined degeneration, which affects the spinocerebellar tracts, lateral corticospinal tracts, and dorsal columns. Lower motoneuron dysfunction would be atypical.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculopathy is an immune-mediated demyelinating condition affecting the spinal nerve roots. Patients typically present with relapsing-remitting or gradually progressive symmetric sensory dysfunction and muscle weakness with depressed or absent deep tendon reflexes that affects the proximal and distal lower extremities simultaneously and may also affect the upper extremities.

text:
A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of progressive weakness of his legs during the past week. He has chronic kidney disease and receives dialysis three times weekly via a percutaneous tunneled catheter. Last week, he had an episode of sweating and chills. His temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 100/min and regular, and blood pressure is 150/92 mm Hg. Examination shows severe tenderness to percussion over the lumbar spine. The catheter site appears normal. There is trace edema of the lower extremities. Muscle strength is 3/5 on flexion and extension of the knees and ankles. Deep tendon reflexes are increased at the patellae. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL
Leukocyte count 19,200/mm³
Segmented neutrophils 90%
Bands 5%
Eosinophils 1%
Lymphocytes 4%
Platelet count 290,000/mm³
An MRI of the spine shows an epidural abscess at L4. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. In addition to antibiotic therapy and drainage of the abscess, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Epidural abscess is a localized collection of pus in the epidural space between the dura mater and periosteum. It most commonly occurs in the spine but may occur in the brain. Most patients have a history of another preceding or associated (even occult) infection and often have a history of immunocompromise (eg, diabetes mellitus, HIV infection, alcohol use disorder). Epidural abscess is more common in patients with a history of intravenous drug use or indwelling intravenous lines. Infectious organisms usually spread to the epidural space hematogenously, from spinal surgery, or from adjacent osteomyelitis. Staphylococcus aureus is a frequent causative organism. Patients present with signs and symptoms of fever, altered mental status, meningismus, localized back pain, and tenderness to palpation, along with weakness, numbness, and paralysis below the affected spinal level as a result of cord compression. Treatment is with broad spectrum antibiotics and surgical drainage of the abscess. It is also crucial to identify and remove potential sources of bacterial colonization, such as indwelling venous catheters.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Electromyography (Choice B) is useful for the diagnosis of various neuromuscular and inflammatory disorders, such as polymyositis, myasthenia gravis, or amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. This patient's MRI has shown a clear cause of his lower extremity weakness, and further testing for other etiologies is less appropriate than removal of potential sources of bacterial colonization.
Technetium 99m bone scan (Choice C) or bone biopsy (Choice D) may be useful for the diagnosis of osteomyelitis or malignancy of bone. Pursuing further diagnostic testing is less appropriate than removal of potential sources of bacterial colonization.
Lumbar puncture (Choice E) is useful for the diagnosis of meningitis but is not usually necessary for patients with epidural abscess.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Epidural abscess may result from hematogenous dissemination of bacterial or fungal infection to the epidural space between the dura mater and periosteum. It presents with fever, altered mental status, meningismus, localized back pain, and tenderness to palpation, along with weakness, numbness, and paralysis below the affected spinal level. Treatment is with broad spectrum antibiotics, surgical drainage of the abscess, and the identification and removal of any potential sources of bacterial colonization, such as indwelling venous catheters.

text: A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife 30 minutes after a 15-minute episode of inability to speak. He has a 15-year history of poorly controlled hypertension. Three weeks ago, he had a transmural myocardial infarction and underwent placement of a stent in the left anterior descending coronary artery. Current medications include clopidogrel, benazepril, and 81-mg aspirin. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. On arrival, he is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. His pulse is 60/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. A grade 1/6 systolic murmur is heard best at the second left intercostal space. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Carotid duplex ultrasonography shows 40% stenosis of the left carotid artery. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

An embolic ischemic stroke involves an event in which a clot breaks off and travels to the brain, occluding blood flow within a cerebral artery. Emboli can originate from proximal vessels, such as the internal carotid artery, or from an atrial or ventricular thrombus. This patient had a recent myocardial infarction, which predisposes him to a ventricular aneurysm. Ventricular aneurysms are more likely to form in the case of large, anterior myocardial infarctions as a result of obstruction of the left anterior descending coronary artery, as was the case in this patient. A small systolic murmur of mitral regurgitation may be heard. Within the aneurysm, as a result of areas of stagnant blood flow and despite being on clopidogrel, a thrombus can form that can embolize and cause an ischemic stroke. If a patent foramen ovale is present, an embolism may arise from either the right or left side of the heart. This patient also has other risk factors for ischemic stroke including hypertension and atherosclerotic disease. Because his symptoms resolved within less than 24 hours, his embolic event is classified as a transient ischemic attack.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Infarct of the lacunar artery (Choice B) causes ischemia to the deep cerebral white matter, pons, or basal ganglia. A lacunar stroke may present with purely motor symptoms, purely sensory symptoms, ataxic hemiparesis, or dysarthria. Infarcts in these deep penetrating vessels are secondary to hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and hypercholesterolemia. While these risk factors would also predispose to myocardial infarction, this patient's transient ischemic event is most likely caused by an embolism from the left ventricle as opposed to a primary central nervous system infarction.
Intraparenchymal cerebral hemorrhage (Choice C) and subarachnoid hemorrhage (Choice E) are both causes of hemorrhagic stroke and are caused by ruptured intracranial or subarachnoid blood vessels in the setting of hypertension. A CT scan of the head without contrast is typically obtained in the acute setting to confirm the presence of blood, which is shown on imaging as hyperdense material. A transient ischemic attack is not caused by hemorrhage.
Spasm of a cerebral artery (Choice D) is a potential complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage which tends to occur within 4 to 10 days after the initial bleed. It leads to subsequent ischemia in the area supplied by the spasmed artery. It is not the cause of this patient's transient ischemic attack.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: An embolic ischemic stroke is caused by a clot that has broken off from either a proximal vessel or thrombus and has occluded blood flow within a cerebral artery. A ventricular aneurysm after a myocardial infarction can cause a thrombus to form in the left ventricle, increasing the risk for an embolic event.

text:
One month after starting isoniazid and rifampin therapy for pulmonary tuberculosis, a 62-year-old woman develops weakness, fatigue, and paresthesias. Examination shows pallor and diminished light touch sensation in the extremities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 9 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume 78 μm³
Leukocyte count 8000/mm³
Platelet count 210,000/mm³
These symptoms are most likely to respond to treatment with which of the following?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

Isoniazid and rifampin are used in the treatment of primary tuberculosis, which results from an initial exposure to Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Isoniazid binds vitamin B₆ (pyridoxine) which may result in early excretion and inactivation of pyridoxine, and also inhibits pyridoxine phosphokinase, which is necessary to activate it. Pyridoxine deficiency results, of which primary symptoms are peripheral neuropathy and paresthesia. Pyridoxine deficiency limits synthesis of histamine, hemoglobin, and neurotransmitters including epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine, serotonin, and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA). In addition to peripheral neuropathy, deficiency commonly presents with dermatitis, sideroblastic anemia, glossitis, and seizures. These adverse effects may all be avoided by pyridoxine supplementation in all patients taking isoniazid.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Folic acid (Choice A) is converted to tetrahydrofolic acid and is used as a coenzyme in the synthesis of nucleotides and nucleosides. Folate is contained in leafy vegetables and absorbed in the jejunum. Folate deficiency is often seen in patients with malnutrition, alcoholism, and patients taking anti-folate medications (eg, phenytoin, methotrexate). It is not caused by isoniazid or rifampin and is not responsible for this patient's symptoms.
Iron (Choice B) deficiency also presents with microcytic anemia. However, it is not induced by isoniazid or rifampin and its supplementation in this case would not address the symptoms of pyridoxine deficiency.
Vitamin B₁ (thiamine) (Choice C) supplementation is not necessary in patients taking isoniazid or rifampin. Thiamine deficiency causes variable manifestations including dry beriberi (peripheral neuropathy with mental status change), wet beriberi (dilated cardiomyopathy leading to heart failure), and Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia), but not microcytic anemia.
Vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin) (Choice E) supplementation is necessary in vegans and patients with malabsorption (eg, sprue), pernicious anemia, and Crohn disease. It is not needed when taking isoniazid or rifampin. Vitamin B₁₂ deficiency presents with megaloblastic anemia, not microcytic anemia, and subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Isoniazid therapy for latent tuberculosis without sufficient pyridoxine supplementation can lead to pyridoxine deficiency. This is characterized by peripheral neuropathy, dermatitis, sideroblastic anemia, glossitis, and seizures.

text: A 22-year-old man who is a collegiate volleyball player comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of progressive pain and weakness in his right shoulder. There is no history of trauma. He is 196 cm (6 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 95 kg (210 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m². There is significant loss of muscle mass in the dorsal scapula region, especially in the area of the scapular spine. Passive abduction and external rotation of the right shoulder and protraction of the scapula reproduce the pain. There is weakness with resisted external rotation and initiation of abduction. Active range of motion shows full flexion and abduction. Sensory examination shows no cutaneous deficits along the right side of the neck or in the right upper extremity. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: E.

The patient's symptoms and physical examination findings are most consistent with entrapment of the suprascapular nerve. The suprascapular nerve originates from the superior trunk of the brachial plexus and innervates the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles. Nerve compression secondary to traction injury is common among volleyball players. Irritation of the suprascapular nerve results in pain along the course of the nerve and may result in chronic neuropathy with subsequent muscle wasting, atrophy, and degeneration of the infraspinatus and supraspinatus muscles. The supraspinatus is responsible for adduction of the shoulder, and the infraspinatus is responsible for external rotation of the shoulder, which reproduce the pain and disclose weakness in this patient. Initial treatment is conservative with activity modification and physical therapy. Operative intervention may be required.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
Adhesive capsulitis (Choice A) is a common cause of shoulder pain in adults, characterized by severely limited range of motion. Patients typically note a gradual onset of progressive shoulder stiffness and pain. Physical examination would disclose limited range of motion while this patient discloses full range of motion.
Axillary nerve damage (Choice B) results in weakness of the deltoid and teres minor, which abduct and stabilize the shoulder. The axillary nerve gives rise to the superficial lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm, and sensory deficits would be expected if injured.
Brachial plexus palsy (Choice C) describes a range of injury to the nervous system complex that gives rise to the nerves of the upper extremity. The mechanism is usually high-impact trauma or forced abduction of the shoulder. Physical examination typically discloses motor and sensory deficits involving several peripheral nerves.
Herniated cervical disc (Choice D) typically presents with neck pain, occipital headache, and unilateral upper extremity weakness, numbness, and/or pain caused by compression of a cervical nerve root. Like brachial plexus palsy, physical examination typically discloses deficits involving multiple peripheral nerves.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Shoulder pain associated with impaired innervation to the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles is suggestive of suprascapular neuropathy. Causes include mass effect as a result of lesions compressing the nerve sheath, trauma, and traction injury (most commonly seen in volleyball players).

text: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because of a 6-month history of fluctuating symptoms including fatigue, loss of balance, blurred vision, and the sensation of pins and needles over her arms and legs. Her husband says that she has also had mood swings with episodes of uncontrollable crying over the past 2 months. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.2°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 128/86 mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows reduced medial gaze of the left eye and numbness to pinprick over the left distal lower extremities. Romberg sign is present. Babinski sign is present on the left. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: Measurement of serum vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) concentration

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: G.

Multiple sclerosis (MS), the most prevalent immune-mediated demyelinating disease, may present with episodic or progressive neurologic dysfunction. According to the McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis, patients must have evidence of demyelinating lesions that are separated in time and location. This evidence is typically based on subjective symptoms and radiologic studies. Lesions affecting the left medial longitudinal fasciculus (leading to decreased medial gaze of the left eye), the prefrontal cortex or corticobulbar tracts (leading to pseudobulbar affect, or uncontrollable crying), and the major central nervous system (CNS) tracts innervating the extremities (lesions of the corticospinal tract causing Babinski sign, spinothalamic tract causing decreased pinprick, dorsal column-medial lemniscus causing Romberg sign and paresthesia) are likely separated in space. This patient experienced two discrete periods of symptom onset at 6 months and 2 months, illustrating separation in time. MRI typically shows white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular region, cortex, subcortical region, cerebellum, or spine. Oligoclonal bands of immunoglobulins, indicating immune overactivation, can be found in the cerebrospinal fluid but are not necessary for diagnosis. Treatment includes corticosteroids for symptomatic management of acute flares and long-term disease-modifying treatments such as interferon or monoclonal antibodies.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answer: A, B, C, D, E, F, H, and I.
EEG (Choice A) assists in diagnosing seizures. Hypothalamic seizures can lead to uncontrollable crying. However, seizures typically last seconds to minutes; months of persistent sensory and motor dysfunction would be atypical.
Measurement of serum ammonia concentration (Choice B) supports a diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy. Hepatic encephalopathy presents acutely with confusion or stupor along with neurologic symptoms such as asterixis, tremor, myoclonus, and frontal release signs.
Measurement of serum calcium concentration (Choice C) would confirm hyper- or hypocalcemia. Hypercalcemia typically presents with confusion, fatigue, polyuria, constipation, and bone pain. Hypocalcemia features muscle spasms, paresthesias, circumoral numbness, and seizures.
Measurement of serum copper concentration (Choice D) diagnoses Wilson disease, in which copper accumulates in the liver, brain, and eyes. Though mood swings and extrapyramidal symptoms (from copper deposits in the basal ganglia) may occur, months of medial gaze palsy along with sensorimotor dysfunction would be atypical.
Measurement of serum vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) concentration (Choice E) helps in diagnosing subacute combined degeneration, affecting the spinocerebellar tracts, lateral corticospinal tracts, and dorsal columns.
Measurement of urine porphobilinogen concentration (Choice F) assists in diagnosing acute intermittent porphyria, which is characterized by episodic abdominal pain, polyneuropathy, psychological symptoms, and port wine-colored urine.
Serum antinuclear antibody assay (Choice H) can assist in diagnosing autoimmune diseases affecting the CNS and are expressed at decreased concentrations in multiple sclerosis. However, characteristic MRI findings are more specific for MS.
Venereal disease research laboratory (VDRL) test in cerebrospinal fluid (Choice I) assists in diagnosing neurosyphilis, which typically presents with progressive dementia or tabes dorsalis.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease that presents with focal neurologic deficits separated in space and time. Though most patients present with episodic deficits, some present with progressive disease. MRI, which confirms the diagnosis, typically shows white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular region, cortex, subcortical region, cerebellum, or spine.

text: A physician makes a house call for an 87-year-old woman who is unable to come to the office because of difficulty with transportation. Her family reports that she has fallen frequently during the past 6 weeks. The patient says that she has had some difficulty feeling where her feet are but that she is otherwise well. She has not had loss of consciousness. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 4/5 on flexion of the hips and extension of the knees and ankles bilaterally. Proprioception and sensation to vibration are decreased in the lower extremities. Sensation to light touch is intact. She has a slapping gait. Mental status examination shows no abnormalities. The physician counsels the patient about making enhancements to her home to prevent falls. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Measurement of serum vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) concentration

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

Subacute combined degeneration, caused by demyelination of the dorsal columns, lateral corticospinal tracts, and spinocerebellar tracts, is a feature of vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) deficiency. Subacute combined degeneration causes a combination of motor and sensory symptoms including ataxic gait, muscle weakness, paresthesia, and loss of proprioception and vibration sense. The effects of these features may include frequent falls, as in this case. Vitamin B₁₂ deficiency is often a result of inadequate dietary intake, seen in veganism, malabsorption, or pernicious anemia. Occasionally, elderly patients with poor nutrition may develop B₁₂ deficiency. Other manifestations include dementia and megaloblastic anemia. In an elderly patient with an acute or subacute change in behavior or activities of daily living, organic causes such as vitamin deficiency should be ruled out.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
Determination of erythrocyte sedimentation rate (Choice A) may be useful in supporting the diagnosis of an inflammatory process, such as polymyositis, as the cause of this patient's lower extremity weakness. However, erythrocyte sedimentation rate is nonspecific and would not suggest a definitive diagnosis. Furthermore, the patient's symptoms are more consistent with vitamin B₁₂ deficiency than an inflammatory process.
X-rays of the lumbosacral spine (Choice C) may be warranted in a patient with recent falls who is suspected to have fractures or other musculoskeletal pathology. However, it is not useful in diagnosing subacute combined degeneration.
EEG (Choice D) is the test of choice in diagnosing a seizure disorder. While seizures could be a cause of recurrent falls, in this case subacute combined degeneration caused by vitamin B₁₂ deficiency is more likely. An EEG is not helpful in making the diagnosis of vitamin B₁₂ deficiency.
Electromyography (Choice E) and nerve conduction studies are used to support a diagnosis of inflammatory myopathy, such as polymyositis. The motor weakness seen in this patient is caused by the demyelination of the lateral corticospinal tracts rather than damage to the peripheral muscles, so electromyography will not be useful.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Subacute combined degeneration is a feature of vitamin B₁₂ deficiency characterized by a combination of motor and sensory symptoms including ataxic gait, muscle weakness, paresthesia, and loss of proprioception and vibration sense.

text: A 27-year-old nulligravid woman has had café au lait spots and multiple neurofibromas since birth. A diagnosis of neurofibromatosis is made. She is concerned that she might pass this condition on to her children. Her parents and three siblings do not have this disorder. The risk for neurofibromatosis in her children is closest to which of the following?

options.A: 50% for each child, since neurofibromatosis is autosomal dominant
options.B: 25% for each child, since neurofibromatosis is autosomal recessive
options.C: 10% for each child, since neurofibromatosis is controlled by several genes
options.D: No risk, since neurofibromatosis is not a genetic disorder
options.E: No risk, since the patient represents a new mutation

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Neurofibromatosis type I is a genetically inherited disorder caused by a defect in NF1, the gene which encodes neurofibromin on chromosome 17. It follows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance. It is characterized by a constellation of cutaneous manifestations (eg, neurofibromas, plexiform neurofibromas, café au lait spots, and axillary freckling), a variety of central nervous system tumors including optic nerve glioma and iris hamartoma (ie, Lisch nodule), and bony abnormalities such as long bone dysplasia and sphenoid wing dysplasia. Patients are also at higher risk for developing astrocytomas, brainstem gliomas, and soft tissue sarcomas. Because it follows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance, only one allele is needed to develop the condition. Thus, the chance of this patient passing on the dominant allele containing the mutation on chromosome 17 is 50% for each child.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
25% for each child (Choice B) would be the chance of a child developing an autosomal recessive disease if both his or her parents were carriers. The defect in NF1 that causes neurofibromatosis type I is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
10% for each child (Choice C) could be the chance of a developing a disease which is controlled by several genes, or for which there is variable penetrance or expressivity, but neurofibromatosis type I is caused by a defect in a single gene, NF1, located on chromosome 17.
No risk for developing neurofibromatosis (Choice D) is incorrect as the disease is a genetic disorder. While the patient likely does represent a new mutation (Choice E) given that her parents are unaffected, this does not change the patient's risk for passing on her mutation to her children.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Neurofibromatosis type I is a genetically inherited disorder caused by a defect in NF1, the gene which encodes neurofibromin on chromosome 17. It follows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance. The chance of an affected patient having an affected child is 50%.

text: A 57-year-old man comes to the physician 2 hours after the sudden onset of visual loss in his right eye. He has no history of similar episodes. He has a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and hypertension treated with lisinopril. He sustained a myocardial infarction 2 years ago. His pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 170/96 mm Hg. Findings on right funduscopic examination are shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

This patient's presenting signs and symptoms of abrupt, painless vision loss and a pale fundus with normal foveal color are consistent with a diagnosis of central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO). CRAO commonly occurs as a result of embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery but may occur secondary to vasculitis, such as in giant cell arteritis (GCA). Patients may have a history of preceding amaurosis fugax. Examination shows profound diminishment of visual acuity, a relative afferent pupillary defect, and a pale fundus with preserved foveal color. CRAO is a medical emergency, as it an ischemic stroke of the ophthalmic vasculature and indicates a high risk for associated ischemic stroke to the brain. Patients who present with CRAO should be admitted to a stroke center for emergent evaluation, including a search for a possible embolic source with echocardiography and duplex ultrasonography of the carotid arteries. A careful history and laboratory evaluation of erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serum concentration of C-reactive protein, and platelet count should be performed to evaluate for possible GCA. There is no proven medical or surgical therapy for CRAO, and the visual prognosis is poor.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, E, F, G, H, I, and J.
Acute angle-closure glaucoma (Choice A) typically presents with pain, conjunctival injection, a fixed, mid-dilated pupil, increased intraocular pressure, and no visible fundus abnormalities. If acute angle-closure glaucoma is not treated promptly it may result in profound, irreversible vision loss.
Bacterial conjunctivitis (Choice B) presents with pain, conjunctival injection, and purulent discharge.
Cataract (Choice C) is an age-related opacification of the crystalline lens and presents with slow vision loss over years that is more pronounced in dim lighting.
Diabetic retinopathy (Choice E) results from microvascular damage and retinal ischemia and is characterized by microaneurysms, retinal hemorrhage, retinal nerve fiber layer infarctions, retinal edema, exudate deposition, and retinal neovascularization. It may cause abrupt vision loss by causing retinal detachment or vitreous hemorrhage.
Endophthalmitis (Choice F) results from bacterial or fungal intraocular infection and presents with severe pain, conjunctival injection, hypopyon, and vitritis.
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (Choice G) presents with headache, nausea, vomiting, tinnitus, and transient visual obscuration or blurry vision secondary to increased intracranial pressure.
Orbital melanoma (Choice H) may occur in the conjunctiva or uveal tract (the iris, ciliary body, or choroid) and presents with decreased visual acuity, floaters, and visual field defects.
Sarcoidosis (Choice I) may present with orbital mass lesions, commonly of the lacrimal gland, or with uveitis characterized by keratic precipitates of the corneal endothelium and granulomatous nodules of the iris.
Temporal arteritis (Choice J), also known as giant cell arteritis, is a vasculitis of medium and large vessels in elderly patients and commonly presents with headache, jaw claudication, proximal muscle stiffness, and sudden, irreversible blindness. Patients typically experience amaurosis fugax prior to the onset of abrupt, irreversible vision loss.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Central retinal artery occlusion frequently occurs secondary to embolic occlusion of the central retinal artery in carotid or cardiac valvular disease. Patients present with acute, painless vision loss and examination findings including diminished visual acuity, relative afferent pupillary defect, and a pale fundus with preserved foveal color. Patients who present with central retinal artery occlusion should be admitted to a stroke center for emergent evaluation.

text: A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician because of facial numbness for 2 weeks. Her symptoms began as a "funny feeling" over a patch of skin on the left side of her chin and have gradually progressed to a "novocaine" sensation. She has had no facial pain or numbness of the gums or oral mucosa. She has a 3-year history of breast cancer metastatic to the lungs treated with tamoxifen. Examination shows supraclavicular adenopathy. Sensation to pinprick, light touch, and temperature is absent over the left side of the chin. Neurologic examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely location of the abnormality?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

Numb chin syndrome, or mental neuropathy, is an indication of bony metastasis to the mandible in this patient with a history of breast cancer. The mandibular (V₃) branch of the trigeminal nerve passes through the bony canal of the mandible, branches to form the inferior alveolar nerve, and branches again proximal to the mental foramen to form the mental nerve. The mental nerve branch affected by mandibular bony metastases provides sensory innervation to the ipsilateral skin of the anterior chin (another unaffected mental nerve branch provides sensory innervation to the ipsilateral lower lip and buccal gingiva). Compression of the mental nerve branch by bony metastasis of the mandible leads to anesthesia of the chin. Numb chin syndrome may indicate systemic malignancy, autoimmune disease (eg, multiple sclerosis), or may be related to local infection, trauma, or dental disease. This patient should receive full-body imaging to evaluate for metastatic spread.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Brachial plexopathy (Choice A) typically presents with weakness, numbness, and/or paresthesia of the shoulder, arm, and/or hand. The chin would be unaffected.
Cavernous sinus thrombosis (Choice B) classically follows an infection or injury involving a structure drained by the cavernous sinus (eg, sinusitis), and results in cranial nerve deficits involving those that traverse the cavernous sinus (generally III, IV, V₁ or V₂, or VI). Classic symptoms include ipsilateral orbit pain, proptosis, periorbital edema, and impairment in extraocular movement, pupil reactivity, or symmetry.
A mass of the cerebellopontine angle (Choice C) (eg, acoustic neuroma, meningioma, glioma) typically affects the vestibulocochlear nerve and may affect other cranial nerves (eg, trigeminal, abducens, facial nerves). The entirety of the trigeminal nerve would be affected rather than the mental branch specifically.
The parietal lobe (Choice E) is a large brain region that processes sensory information and promotes spatial awareness of the body. Damage to the parietal lobe can result in symptoms such as agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and left-right disorientation (dominant hemisphere, known as Gerstmann syndrome) or hemineglect (non-dominant hemisphere).
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Numb chin syndrome, or mental neuropathy, may be caused by systemic malignancy, autoimmune disease, or local dental processes. In numb chin syndrome secondary to malignancy, bony metastasis to the mandible leads to compression of a branch of the mental nerve, resulting in anesthesia of the ipsilateral chin.

text: A 32-year-old woman with AIDS comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of painful burning sensations in her hands and feet. Examination shows no abnormalities. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.E: Topical lidocaine to the affected areas

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Peripheral neuropathy is a frequent finding in patients with HIV and may occur either secondary to direct viral infection of peripheral nerves or secondary to neurotoxicity associated with antiretroviral medications, frequently medications in the nucleoside analog reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) class. HIV-associated neuropathy most frequently presents with symmetric, distal polyneuropathy characterized by sensations of burning, tingling, or numbness in a stocking-glove distribution, as well as reduced vibratory or pinprick sensation. As with other forms of distal polyneuropathy, tricyclic antidepressants such as nortriptyline are effective as first-line treatment. Other effective treatments include gabapentin, pregabalin, lamotrigine, and topical capsaicin or lidocaine.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
Oral fluoxetine (Choice A) is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) and is not typically used as a first-line agent for peripheral neuropathic pain. While some SSRIs, such as duloxetine, may be effective for the treatment of neuropathic pain, fluoxetine is not effective in treating nerve pain.
Oral lorazepam (Choice B) is a short-acting benzodiazepine and is not recommended for the treatment of neuropathic pain because of its significant adverse effects and risk for dependency.
Oral prednisone (Choice D) is a corticosteroid. While corticosteroids, including prednisone, may be effective for some forms of neuropathic pain, this is not an appropriate initial pharmacotherapy for this patient given the significant adverse effects of corticosteroids, which includes immunosuppression.
Topical lidocaine to the affected areas (Choice E) is not the best initial pharmacotherapy for peripheral neuropathy because they are less effective in comparison with systemic oral therapy such as tricyclic antidepressants.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Peripheral neuropathy is a frequent finding in patients with HIV and may occur either secondary to direct viral infection of peripheral nerves or secondary to neurotoxicity associated with antiretroviral medications. Tricyclic antidepressants, such as nortriptyline, are an effective first-line therapy.

text: A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of moderate low back pain. He thinks he may have hurt his back while doing yard work. His pain moderately improves with rest; he has been spending a great amount of time in bed. He has not had other symptoms, weight loss, or a recent infection. He has no history of serious illness. His only medication is ibuprofen for the pain. He does not use intravenous drugs or anabolic steroids. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 102 kg (225 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m². Examination shows mild paraspinal muscle tenderness. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Oxycodone therapy as needed for pain

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

Low back pain is a common presenting complaint and it can be difficult to identify an exact underlying cause. Most nonspecific low back pain is attributed to musculoskeletal injury from exertion, overuse, or trauma. The majority of these cases will resolve with conservative management, which includes activity modification, stretching, over-the-counter analgesics such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), massage, acupuncture, and heat therapy. Certain red flag signs and symptoms should prompt more urgent evaluation for a cause, especially if there is concern for infection, malignancy, or fracture. These include new fevers, intravenous drug use, focal neurologic deficits (such as urinary retention, saddle anesthesia, bowel incontinence which may suggest cauda equina syndrome), progressive lower extremity neurologic symptoms, current or recent cancer history, advanced age, systemic corticosteroid use, and a significant traumatic mechanism. In this young, otherwise healthy patient with a mild inciting event, the next best step in management is continuation of ibuprofen therapy.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Bed rest for 10 days (Choice A) is not recommended for acute low back pain, as patients tend to have increased pain and a slower return to normal function. Activity modification for symptom relief should be advised, with encouragement to continue normal activities of daily living and non-exacerbating exertion.
X-ray of the lumbar spine (Choice B) and MRI of the lumbar spine (Choice C) should be reserved for patients who present with red flag signs and symptoms to evaluate for evidence of fracture, infection, or malignancy. For otherwise healthy patients with back pain that has persisted for less than 4 weeks, imaging studies do not generally change management.
Oxycodone therapy as needed for pain (Choice E) should be reserved for severe or refractory cases of acute low back pain.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Acute low back pain is a common presenting complaint in multiple medical settings. General management for pain persisting less than 4 weeks is conservative with over-the-counter analgesics, activity modification, and nonpharmacologic pain relief options. Initial imaging is not typically recommended in the absence of signs or symptoms suggestive of infection, malignancy, spinal cord compromise, or vertebral fracture.

text: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 3 weeks after operative repair of a circle of Willis berry aneurysm. Her husband says that her behavior has changed since the operation. She is unmotivated and just sits around all day like a "bump on a log." She has not had depressed mood. Examination shows psychomotor retardation. She is oriented to person, place, and time. She is cooperative and her speech is fluent and grammatical, but there is little spontaneous speech during conversation. Which of the following cerebral cortical regions is most likely affected?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

The patient is exhibiting symptoms of frontal lobe dysfunction. The frontal lobe is the largest lobe of the cerebral cortex, separated from the parietal lobe by the central sulcus. The major areas of the frontal lobe are the primary motor cortex, premotor cortex, prefrontal cortex, Broca area, and the frontal eye fields. Symptoms depend on the location of the lesion. Involvement of the primary motor cortex and premotor cortex result in impaired motor function of the contralateral side of the body and psychomotor retardation. Disruption of Broca area may cause expressive aphasia with retention of language comprehension. Lesions of the frontal eye fields result in ipsilateral deviation of the eyes. Dysfunction of the prefrontal cortex can result in disinhibition, apathy, personality changes, aggressive behavior, and impairments in judgement, concentration, and orientation, as exhibited by this patient. Patients should be evaluated for other medical causes of cognitive impairment, such as vitamin B₁₂ (cobalamin) deficiency, hypothyroidism, syphilis infection, and intoxication. In this patient with a recent invasive procedure, CT scan or MRI of the brain is likely to show the diagnosis.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.
Insular (Choice B) cortex lesions may result in impairments in emotional and/or sensory processing, motor control, and speech. Patients may display a progressive expressive dysphasia characterized by non-fluent and grammatically incoherent speech.
Occipital (Choice C) lobe lesions typically present with difficulties in processing visual sensory inputs. Patients may complain of visual field cuts depending on the precise location of the lesion.
Parietal (Choice D) lobe lesions manifest differently depending on the side affected. Dominant parietal lobe lesions, most commonly on the left side, may result in Gerstmann syndrome, characterized by agraphia, aphasia, agnosia, acalculia, and right-left confusion. Nondominant parietal lobe lesions typically manifest with hemineglect of the contralateral side.
Temporal (Choice E) lobe lesions may involve Wernicke area, the limbic system, and/or the amygdala. Wernicke area lesions result in receptive aphasia with loss of language comprehension. Disruption of the limbic system results in dysfunctional emotional processing, and may present with excessive fear, anxiety, and behavioral changes.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: The frontal lobe contains the primary motor cortex, premotor cortex, Broca area, frontal eye fields, and prefrontal cortex, which is involved in the regulation of behavior, inhibition, and other executive functions. Injury to the frontal lobe may result in personality or behavior changes, disinhibited behavior, expressive aphasia, and motor dysfunction.

text:
A 10-day-old newborn is brought to the emergency department by ambulance because of a seizure that involved clonic jerking of the left arm. He had been well until 2 days ago when he began sleeping somewhat more but continued to feed well. Pregnancy, labor, and delivery were uncomplicated. Vital signs are within normal limits. He is difficult to awaken but moves all extremities when stimulated. He is hypotonic but hyperreflexic. Babinski sign is present. During examination, he has a clonic seizure of the right lower extremity. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows:
Glucose 55 mg/dL
Protein 110 mg/dL
Leukocyte count 85/mm³
Erythrocyte count 860/mm³
A Gram stain is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: F.

Neonatal herpes simplex virus (HSV) encephalitis is the most likely diagnosis in this 10-day old infant with new onset seizures, a normal head CT scan, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showing increased protein and leukocyte count with a low-normal glucose concentration, which is consistent with aseptic (nonbacterial) meningitis. Further suggestive is the presence of erythrocytes in the CSF which is often seen in herpetic encephalitis. Most cases of HSV encephalitis are caused by HSV-2, although infection with HSV-1 occurs and is treated in the same way. Most patients acquire the virus via transmission from the mother, often during the third trimester, with encephalitis developing usually within a week. Symptoms are nonspecific and include fever, lethargy, poor feeding, and occasionally seizures or focal neurologic deficits. Concomitant oral lesions may occur, but their absence does not rule out the diagnosis. The CSF findings include increased protein, lymphocytic pleocytosis, and a normal or low-normal glucose concentration. In many instances the causative virus can be identified by polymerase chain reaction (PCR) of the CSF, although empiric therapy with intravenous acyclovir is warranted prior to diagnosis. Failure to recognize and treat infection can result in organ dysfunction and death, or long-term neurologic sequelae.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, and E.
Congenital infection with cytomegalovirus (Choice A) is associated with petechiae, bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, jaundice, microcephaly, hepatosplenomegaly, and hypotonia. The patient lacks these other features. Intracranial calcifications may be present on CT scan.
Congenital infection with syphilis (Choice B) presents typically within five weeks with hepatomegaly, maculopapular rash, rhinitis, and skeletal abnormalities including periostitis or bone demineralization. CSF findings may show pleocytosis and increased protein but would be positive for Treponema pallidum.
Congenital infection with toxoplasmosis (Choice C) may present with chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, and seizures. Toxoplasma gondii can be detected in the CSF. Intracranial calcifications may be present on CT scan.
Neonatal Escherichia coli meningitis (Choice D) may present with fever, lethargy, and hypotonia, and CSF findings would include a neutrophilic pleocytosis with a decreased glucose concentration. Treatment is with ampicillin if the organism is sensitive, or with a cephalosporin combined with gentamicin.
Neonatal group B streptococcus (GBS) meningitis (Choice E) presents similarly to E. coli meningitis but is more common in patients born to mothers who are colonized with GBS who have not received prophylactic treatment. Colonization is usually detected and treated prior to delivery, and if the status is unknown, prophylactic antibiotics are given.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: HSV encephalitis should be suspected in neonates who develop fever, lethargy, and/or neurologic symptoms, including focal deficits or seizures shortly after birth. A history of HSV infection in the mother may or may not be present. Evaluation of the CSF typically shows an increased protein concentration with a lymphocytic pleocytosis but normal glucose concentrations. Therapy is with intravenous acyclovir.

text: A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of increasingly frequent headaches during the past 3 months. The headaches are throbbing, occur only on the right side, and are associated with nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound. She has had one or two headaches yearly since the age of 17 years but now has two or three weekly. She is a schoolteacher and has had no changes in work, lifestyle, or environment. Four months ago, conjugated estrogen therapy was begun for treatment of menopausal symptoms. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 135/80 mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Butalbital therapy combined with acetaminophen and caffeine

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: C.

Migraine is a common condition that presents with recurrent primary headaches. Migraine headaches are typically severe, unilateral, throbbing, and associated with nausea, vomiting, photophobia, and phonophobia. The pain is usually located behind or adjacent to the eye. Some patients additionally experience an aura before or during the headache, which may present with vision loss followed by the perception of scintillating shapes or lines and/or paresthesia. Migraine triggers include stress, menstruation and changes in hormonal concentrations, weather changes, fasting, and alcohol. Sleep may improve migraines. Patients who experience migraines associated with conjugated estrogen therapy may require discontinuation of therapy or a reduction in dose. Migraines are postulated to arise from deficient serotonin inhibition of descending pain pathways. Acute migraine treatment may include triptans and/or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). For patients with disabling or frequent migraines, prophylactic treatment is also recommended and includes β-adrenergic blockers, antidepressants such as tricyclic antidepressants and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and anticonvulsants.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.
CT scan of the head (Choice A) is unnecessary given a likely cause for the patient's migraines and absence of any neurologic abnormalities.
Butalbital therapy combined with acetaminophen and caffeine (Choice B) may be appropriate in the treatment of a primary migraine, however, it carries risk given the adverse effects and dependency associated with barbiturates. Removal of likely offending agents is a more appropriate first step for the treatment of this patient's likely-triggered migraine.
EEG (Choice D) is useful for the diagnosis of seizure disorders but is rarely useful for the evaluation or treatment of migraine.
Lumbar puncture (Choice E) is useful for the diagnosis of meningitis or idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). While IIH may be associated with hormonal therapy, this patient's symptoms of unilateral, throbbing headache, photophobia and phonophobia are more suggestive of migraine. The headaches associated with IIH are more commonly holocephalic and associated with transient visual obscurations and pulsatile tinnitus.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Migraines are typically unilateral, pulsating headaches that are associated with nausea, vomiting, photophobia, and phonophobia, and may present with visual or sensory aura. Changes in hormone concentrations are a common migraine trigger. Patients who experience migraines associated with conjugated estrogen therapy may require discontinuation of therapy or a reduction in dose.

text: A 67-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of a 1-month history of deafness, ringing in both ears, dizziness, and difficulty with balance. He also has had an occasional sensation that the room is spinning. He was discharged from the hospital 21 days ago after prolonged treatment for pneumonia and sepsis with intravenous ceftriaxone and gentamicin. He has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and congestive heart failure. He has a history of tuberculosis 10 years ago. Medications include digoxin, metformin, and losartan. His pulse is 84/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 140/85 mm Hg. Examination shows nystagmus on lateral gaze bilaterally. There is severe loss of hearing bilaterally. Air conduction is greater than bone conduction. Results of Weber test are normal. Muscle strength, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory findings are normal. His gait is broad-based, and he is unable to walk in tandem. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
