explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Cauda equina syndrome occurs as a result of compression of the spinal nerve roots of the cauda equina and presents with lower extremity numbness, weakness, hyporeflexia, perineal anesthesia, fecal incontinence, and urinary retention. Diagnosis is made with MRI and definitive treatment includes emergent surgical decompression with lumbar laminectomy.

text: A 32-year-old man comes to the physician 2 hours after the onset of dizziness, nausea, and unsteadiness. He had a whiplash neck injury 2 years ago and is still receiving monthly chiropractic manipulations. His voice is hoarse. There is left ptosis and nystagmus. The left pupil is 2 mm smaller than the right. There is decreased sensation to pain over the left side of the face and right extremities. He has ataxia on the left. Mental status examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following arteries is most likely to be occluded?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: G.

This patient likely has an occlusion of the left vertebral artery leading to vertebrobasilar insufficiency. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency can be caused by ischemia in patients with poor sympathetic regulation or thromboembolism commonly originating from an atherosclerotic plaque in or dissection of the vertebral artery (known as a posterior circulation stroke). A thrombus of the vertebral artery commonly forms from atherosclerotic disease but may also form as a result of an intimal defect from trauma or vertebral artery dissection, a known complication of chiropractic manipulation. The brain stem, cerebellum, and occipital lobes are commonly affected. Patients typically present with vestibulocerebellar signs (eg, ataxia, dysmetria, dizziness, imbalance, vertigo, vomiting, nystagmus), ipsilateral cranial nerve dysfunction (eg, dysphagia, dysarthria, vertical or horizontal gaze palsies), contralateral hemiparesis (caused by corticospinal tract damage), contralateral impairment in pain and temperature sensation (caused by spinothalamic tract damage), and/or contralateral homonymous hemianopsia with macular sparing (caused by posterior cerebral artery involvement). In posterior circulation strokes affecting the lateral pons, Horner syndrome may occur, which presents with unilateral ptosis and miosis (pupillary constriction) and ipsilateral facial anhidrosis. If no hemorrhage is shown on noncontrast CT scan of the head, acute management of posterior circulation strokes may involve tissue plasminogen activator therapy or thrombectomy. In dissection, antiplatelet and anticoagulant administration is indicated to prevent conversion to a thromboembolic stroke if none has yet occurred.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, F, H, I, J, K, L, and M.
The basilar artery (Choice A) is supplied by the vertebral arteries. Compared to vertebral artery occlusion, basilar artery occlusion may present with more acute and severe corticospinal and corticobulbar dysfunction, leading to locked-in syndrome. The vertebral arteries are more vulnerable than the basilar artery to chiropractic manipulation-related intimal injury.
The left and right anterior cerebral arteries (ACA) (Choices B and H) supply the medial frontal lobe (primary motor and sensory cortices), which controls the motor and sensory function of the lower extremities. Occlusion of the ACA leads to contralateral hemiparesis and contralateral sensory deficit of the lower extremities.
The left and right internal carotid arteries (ICA) (Choices C and I) supply the left and right anterior and middle cerebral arteries (MCA) (Choices D and J). The MCA supplies the lateral frontal lobe (primary motor and sensory cortices), anterior parietal lobe, and temporal lobe. Occlusion of the ICA or MCA leads to contralateral hemiparesis and contralateral sensory deficit of the face and upper extremities.
The left and right ophthalmic arteries (Choices E and K) supply structures of the orbit. Occlusion may lead to blindness.
The left and right posterior cerebral arteries (PCA) (Choices F and L) supply the posterior parietal and occipital lobes along with the thalamus and midbrain. Occlusion of the right PCA typically leads to left homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing and spatial disorientation.
Right vertebral artery (Choice M) dissection or occlusion would result in ipsilateral Horner syndrome and right-sided cranial nerve deficits with crossed sensory deficit in the contralateral body, which this patient does not demonstrate.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Vertebral artery dissection commonly leads to thromboembolic strokes of the posterior circulation, known as vertebrobasilar insufficiency. Patients typically present with vestibulocerebellar signs, ipsilateral cranial nerve dysfunction, contralateral hemiparesis, contralateral decrease in pain and temperature sensation, contralateral homonymous hemianopsia with macular sparing, and/or Horner syndrome.

text: A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-month history of headaches and blurred vision. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Menses occur at irregular 2- to 10-week intervals. She has had an 18-kg (40-lb) weight gain during the past 7 months. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and now weighs 86 kg (190 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m². Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Visual field testing shows no abnormalities. Funduscopic examination shows blurred optic discs; there are no spontaneous venous pulsations. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A CT scan of the head shows small lateral ventricles. On lumbar puncture, the opening pressure is 290 mm H₂O; cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis shows no leukocytes or erythrocytes. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of these findings?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

Impaired resorption of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is the primary mechanism behind pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH). IIH is characterized by signs of increased intracranial pressure including headache, papilledema, and increased opening pressure on lumbar puncture without any underlying pathology to cause such findings on neuroimaging. It is primarily seen in young women who are overweight. Although there is no malignant cause underlying the increased intracranial pressure, it may cause debilitating headaches and permanent vision loss. Headaches that are secondary to increased intracranial pressure often are worse when lying down and relieved by standing. The most common risk factors for the development of pseudotumor cerebri include female gender and obesity. However, hypervitaminosis A from increased dietary intake is another potential cause of IIH. This can be from excessive dietary intake or from the use of retinoid medications such as isotretinoin or all-trans retinoic acid. Other medications associated with IIH include tetracyclines, danazol, and growth hormone.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Aqueductal stenosis (Choice A) causes dilation of the lateral and third ventricles and is a cause of hydrocephalus in children. Idiopathic increased intracranial pressure presents with symptoms of increased intracranial pressure (nausea, vomiting, positional headache) without hydrocephalus.
Cerebellar astrocytoma (Choice B) is a primary central nervous system tumor seen most often in children and a potential cause of secondary intracranial hypertension and hydrocephalus if it obstructs the flow of CSF. IIH is characterized by increased intracranial pressure but not hydrocephalus.
Choroid plexus papilloma (Choice C), a hamartoma of the choroid plexus and rare intracranial tumor, is a cause of communicating hydrocephalus by increasing the production of CSF. It most commonly presents in a young child with headache secondary to increased intracranial pressure.
Lateral venous sinus thrombosis (Choice E) may be induced by an infection of facial skin, orbit, or nasal sinuses which causes a septic thrombus to form. The most common presenting symptom is acute onset of headache, though orbital pain, edema, visual disturbances, fever, and mental status changes are also seen. This young woman with obesity, chronic headache, and signs of increased intracranial pressure suggests IIH instead.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Impaired resorption of CSF is the mechanism behind pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), which is characterized by headache, papilledema, and increased opening pressure on lumbar puncture without any underlying pathology to cause such findings on neuroimaging. It is primarily seen in young women who are overweight.

text: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after a motor vehicle collision in which he was the unrestrained front seat passenger; he lost consciousness momentarily. Examination shows a 2 x 2-cm area of soft-tissue swelling over the forehead, a small abrasion on the left cheek, and an abrasion on the right elbow. Neurologic findings are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

Mild traumatic brain injury, also known as a concussion, commonly results in the loss of consciousness, confusion, amnesia, headache, dizziness, nausea, and/or vomiting minutes after the injury. A concussion can be caused by direct or indirect blunt head trauma and typically causes self-limited brief neurologic impairment, such as the loss of consciousness, repetitive questioning, or confusion. Symptoms that commonly develop within the following days include mood disturbances, photophonophobia, trouble concentrating, and/or insomnia. Patients do not have focal neurologic symptoms such as limb weakness or vision changes that would indicate traumatic vascular injury or intracranial hemorrhage. All patients with suspected concussion should undergo a thorough neurologic examination, and a subset of at-risk patients should receive neuroimaging. All patients should be monitored closely for neurologic deterioration, which may indicate a developing intracranial hematoma. To prevent cumulative brain injury (eg, from a second concussion), patients with concussion should return to their usual activities in a graded fashion, beginning with the return to school and without return to sports until cleared by a physician.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, and F.
A cervical spine fracture (Choice B) could present with neck pain, altered mental status, focal neurologic deficits, and decreased neck mobility.
Closed head injury (Choice C) refers to blunt head trauma with no object penetrating the skull and dura mater, and encompasses a variety of injuries, such as hematomas, skull fractures, intracranial hemorrhage, or traumatic brain injury. This patient lost consciousness momentarily and has no other apparent neurologic deficits, making concussion a more specific diagnosis.
Epidural hematoma (Choice D) classically occurs after injury to the middle meningeal artery and can present with an initial lucid interval followed by deterioration of mental status as a result of arterial bleeding and hematoma expansion. Epidural hematomas present on CT scan as lens-shaped, bi-convex hyperdense collections abutting the skull.
Facial bone fracture (Choice E) can occur as a result of direct blunt trauma to the face and can be associated with pain and ecchymosis. Patients can present with a variety of signs or symptoms depending on which facial bone is fractured, including facial deformity, midface instability, malocclusion, nerve entrapment, or diplopia.
A frontal skull fracture (Choice F) is uncommon as a result of the hardness of the bone, but can occur after severe, high-impact blunt head injury and can be associated with intracranial bleeding. It can present with swelling, tenderness, a palpable skull defect or crepitus, and can be associated with altered mental status.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: A concussion can be caused by direct or indirect blunt head trauma and typically causes self-limited brief neurologic impairment, such as the loss of consciousness, repetitive questioning, or confusion.

text: A previously healthy 6-month-old girl is brought to the emergency department by ambulance because of difficulty breathing. Her mother says that she fell from the couch to a carpeted floor yesterday. She cried immediately and was fine until several hours later when she could not be aroused and seemed to be gasping for breath. Her mother noticed some emesis in the bed. On arrival, she is lethargic. Her temperature is 36.5°C(97.7°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 36/min, and blood pressure is 92/60 mm Hg. Examination shows a bulging anterior fontanelle. There are bilateral retinal hemorrhages. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. A CT scan of the head shows blood along the falx. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.M: Skull fracture over the middle meningeal artery

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: L.

Severe intracranial injury with a history inconsistent with the severity of the patient's physical findings raises suspicion for abusive head trauma and non-accidental trauma. Patients with abusive head trauma can present with encephalopathy, apnea, seizures, and retinal hemorrhages. The mechanism of abusive head trauma is typically blunt force or shaking, which results in an acceleration-deceleration force on the infant. Infants are vulnerable to deceleration injury because of several factors. The large head and weak neck musculature result in greater movement of the brain in the intracranial space when acted upon by acceleration and deceleration force such as in shaking. In addition, the infant skull is thin, allowing forces to transfer through the skull to the brain. Findings on CT scan associated with non-accidental head trauma include subdural hemorrhage, especially multiple hemorrhages or those in an interhemispheric or posterior fossa location, hypoxic-ischemic injury, and cerebral edema.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, M, and N.
Basilar skull fractures (Choice A) typically occur after high-impact blunt head injury and would be unlikely in this patient. Patients can present with hemotympanum, detected RBC in analysis of cerebrospinal fluid, bloody otorrhea or rhinorrhea, mastoid and periorbital ecchymosis, and cranial nerve palsy.
Caput succedaneum (Choice B) refers to extraperiosteal swelling or ecchymosis on a neonate's scalp with poorly defined margins following vaginal delivery. It is a common and benign condition that resolves spontaneously.
A cephalhematoma (Choice C) occurs typically during prolonged or instrumental delivery because of rupture of blood vessels causing hemorrhage beneath the periosteum, resulting in swelling and ecchymosis with margins defined by suture lines. It can be complicated by jaundice, anemia, and hypotension if bleeding is severe.
A cervical spine fracture (Choice D) could present with neck pain, altered mental status, focal neurologic deficits, and decreased neck mobility.
A depressed skull fracture (Choice E) or frontal skull fracture (Choice I) can occur after severe, high-impact blunt head injury and can be associated with intracranial bleeding. However, interhemispheric and retinal hemorrhages are more specific for shaken baby syndrome.
Diffuse axonal injury (Choice F) can occur with rapid acceleration-deceleration injury but is characterized by microscopic widespread axonal separation that is not typically apparent on a CT scan.
Epidural hematoma (Choice G) classically occurs after injury to the middle meningeal artery such as from an overlying temporal bone fracture (Choice M) and can present with an initial lucid interval followed by deterioration of mental status as a result of arterial bleeding and hematoma expansion. Epidural hematomas present on CT scan as lens-shaped, bi-convex hyperdense collections abutting the skull.
Facial bone fracture (Choice H) can occur as a result of direct blunt trauma to the face and can be associated with pain and ecchymosis. It would be apparent on CT scan.
Intracerebral hematoma (Choice J) can occur secondary to trauma, aneurysms, arteriovenous malformations, tumors, or hypertension. It would appear on CT scan as an area of intraparenchymal hyperdensity, whereas interhemispheric hemorrhage is more specific for shaken baby syndrome.
An orbital blow-out fracture (Choice K) is characterized by orbital pain, enophthalmos, limited eye movement, and diplopia in direct orbital trauma.
A subdural hematoma (Choice N) can occur after blunt head trauma and would appear as a crescent-shaped fluid collection abutting the internal surface of the skull not bound by suture lines on imaging.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Patients with abusive head trauma can present with encephalopathy, apnea, seizures, and retinal hemorrhages. The mechanism of abusive head trauma is typically by blunt force or shaking.

text: A 67-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 7-month history of progressive bilateral leg pain. She describes bilateral buttock, posterior thigh, and calf pain that begins after standing or walking for 10 minutes. It is relieved in 2 minutes by bending forward at the waist or sitting. She has coronary artery disease and underwent coronary artery bypass grafting 2 years ago. Neurologic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Doppler flow studies of the lower extremities

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: D.

The patient is presenting with symptoms suggestive of spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication. Spinal stenosis describes narrowing of the central canal, neural foramen, or lateral recesses of the spine with subsequent nerve compression and ischemia. Common causes include degenerative arthritis, spondyloarthropathies, degenerative disc disease, hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum, and osteophyte formation in the facet joints. Neurogenic claudication describes low back pain with radiation to the buttocks and lower extremities that results from increased nerve compression, as may occur with extension of the spine during standing and walking downhill. Reducing narrowing within the spinal column with spinal flexion as during sitting or walking uphill tends to relieve the pain. Diagnostic evaluation involves x-ray imaging followed by MRI of the lumbar spine.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
Carotid duplex ultrasonography (Choice A) is recommended to screen for atherosclerotic plaque in patients with a known cardiovascular disease history or significant risk factors. If a stroke or transient ischemic attack were suspected as the cause of the patient's neurologic symptoms, carotid duplex ultrasonography should be performed to evaluate for intervenable lesions. The exacerbating and alleviating factors in this case are more suggestive of spinal stenosis.
Doppler flow studies of the lower extremities (Choice B) are useful in distinguishing neurogenic claudication from claudication caused by peripheral vascular disease. Vascular claudication is typically relieved by rest as opposed to postural changes.
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies (Choice C) can be used to confirm the presence of radiculopathy caused by nerve root compression or evaluate for other myopathy or neuropathy as the cause of the patient's symptoms. These tests are reasonable if the diagnosis is still in doubt after the initial imaging studies. MRI is the most appropriate next step.
CT myelography (Choice E) is recommended for evaluating the bony structures of the spine in the setting of prior spine surgery, where hardware may cause significant artifact on MRI, or for patients who are unable to obtain an MRI for other reasons.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Lower extremity pain that is exacerbated by extension of the spinal column and relieved by flexion is suggestive of neurogenic claudication resulting from spinal stenosis. Diagnostic testing should include x-rays and/or MRI of the spine.

text: A 37-year-old woman has had throbbing right-sided headaches that began gradually 6 weeks ago and have increased in frequency and severity. The headaches typically begin in the morning shortly after arising, last up to 1 hour, and occur several times daily. Acetaminophen has provided partial relief. She is irritable and has been unable to keep up daily activities. She appears upset. Neurologic examination shows a persistent attention deficit and left arm drift. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: A.

This patient's examination findings of attention deficit (likely neglect) and left arm drift are suggestive of the presence of a contralateral upper motoneuron lesion, which, in conjunction with her symptoms of new chronic headache, is suggestive of a brain tumor. Hemispatial neglect often occurs as a result of lesions of the parietal cortex and may also occur in stroke. Similarly, left arm drift may be suggestive of an upper motoneuron lesion in a variety of locations, such as the contralateral frontal cortex, cerebellum, or pyramidal tract. Collectively, this patient's findings localize her lesion to the right parietal cortex.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K and L.
Cerebral hemorrhage (Choice B) may present with abrupt and acute onset of headache and focal weakness or numbness.
Cerebral nerve root impingement (Choice C) may cause radiculopathy and present with weakness, numbness, paresthesia, and pain in the dermatomal and myotomal distribution that correlates to the affected root.
Cluster headache (Choice D) is a primary headache that typically presents with short-lived, unilateral, severe pain in the orbital, supraorbital, or temporal region of the head and is accompanied by ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (eg, miosis, ptosis, lacrimation, nasal congestion).
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (Choice E) presents commonly in younger, overweight females taking vitamin A, oral contraceptives, or danazol. It is usually associated with a holocephalic headache and blurry vision.
Increased intracranial pressure (Choice F) may occur as a result of brain tumors, hemorrhage, or may be idiopathic, and presents with headache, nausea, vomiting, and papilledema.
Migraines (Choice G) are a common cause of headache in adult patients. They may present with visual aura of scintillating scotoma or fortification spectra but do not typically cause hemineglect or arm drift. They are generally less than 48 to 72 hours in total duration.
Occipital neuralgia (Choice H) is a rare cause of headache and presents with retro-orbital or occipital pain. It does not typically present with neurologic signs.
Subarachnoid hemorrhage (Choice I) presents with severe headache of acute onset, nausea, vomiting, and meningismus, and may rapidly lead to coma and death. This patient's subacute presentation over 6 weeks is not consistent with a diagnosis of subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Subdural hematoma (Choice J) may result from head trauma and hemorrhage from bridging veins in the subdural space, and presents with subacute onset headache, nausea, vomiting, and may present with focal neurologic deficits or seizures as a result of mass effect.
Temporal arteritis (Choice K) is a vasculitis of medium and large vessels in elderly patients and commonly presents with systemic symptoms, headache, jaw claudication, proximal muscle stiffness, and sudden, irreversible blindness.
Tension-type headache (Choice L) typically presents with bilateral dull, pressure-like or band-like pain across the frontal and temporal areas. Tension-type headache does not present with neurologic signs.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Mass lesions of the right parietal cortex may present with left hemineglect and left arm drift. Patients may also present with headache, nausea, and vomiting. Mass lesions typically present with a chronic or subacute course with progressive, non-remitting symptoms over a period of weeks or months.

text: A 77-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-year history of increasingly severe pain, swelling, and numbness of her left arm and shoulder; during the past 8 weeks, she has had difficulty moving her left arm because of these symptoms. Fifteen years ago, she underwent a left radical mastectomy followed by radiation therapy of the chest wall for breast cancer. There has been no evidence of recurrence of breast cancer. She has hypertension treated with atenolol. Her only other medication is ibuprofen as needed for pain. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no neck, axillary, or chest wall masses. There is 2+ pitting edema of the entire left upper extremity. Examination of the right upper extremity shows no abnormalities. Radial and ulnar pulses are 2+ and equal bilaterally. Sensation to pinprick and motor function of the left upper extremity are absent. A photograph of the chest wall and left upper extremity is shown. An MRI of the left upper extremity and shoulder shows fibrosis but no masses. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.A: Entrapment of a cervical nerve root

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: B.

Radiation-induced brachial plexopathy is a potential complication of radiation to the brachial plexus, most commonly occurring after radiation therapy for breast or lung cancer. Radiation damages the brachial plexus and presents with symptoms of brachial neuritis, including pain, paresthesia, and weakness in the distribution of the affected nerves that may occur months or years after the initial insult. In this scenario, the radiation has likely affected the C5 and C6 nerve roots (called Erb Palsy), leading to dysfunction of the deltoid, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and biceps brachii muscles, resulting in impaired upper extremity abduction, lateral rotation, flexion, and supination. Consequently, the patient's upper extremity hangs near the body in a medially rotated, extended, and pronated position as shown in the photograph. Lymphedema of the upper extremity can also result from radiation-induced fibrosis of the lymph nodes and is often associated with radiation-induced brachial plexopathy. Neoplastic brachial plexopathy should be ruled out with MRI. Treatment is supportive and includes brachial decompression surgery, pain management, and physical and occupational therapy.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
Entrapment of a cervical nerve root (Choice A), which commonly results from cervical spinal stenosis (Choice D), leads to weakness, numbness, paresthesia, and pain in the dermatomal and myotomal distribution that correlates to the affected root. This patient shows sensorimotor dysfunction that crosses dermatome and myotome boundaries, indicating that multiple nerve roots are affected.
Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (Choice C), or complex regional pain syndrome, presents with regional pain that is out of the proportion to the insult. Patients may also experience edema or abnormal sensory or motor findings in the region. This patient instead possesses a pattern of findings (Erb palsy and lymphedema) consistent with radiation-induced brachial plexopathy, indicating her symptoms are not out of proportion to the insult.
Syringomyelia (Choice E) features a cystic cavity in the central cervical and/or thoracic spinal cord, which affects midline structures such as the anterior white commissure (decussation of the spinothalamic tracts) and the anterior horns (cell bodies of lower motoneurons). Patients with syringomyelia may present with deficits in pain and temperature sensation and a lower motoneuron-pattern of dysfunction in the bilateral upper extremities.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Radiation-induced brachial plexopathy is a potential complication of radiation therapy for breast cancer. Patients typically present with pain, paresthesia, and weakness in the distribution of the affected nerves that may occur months or years after the initial insult. Associated lymphedema is also common.

text: An 82-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after he collapsed while walking in his garden. He has hypercholesterolemia. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 60 years. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 96/min and irregularly irregular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. He is awake but is unable to speak due to facial and tongue paralysis. He is able to blink his eyes and move his eyes vertically on command. He cannot move his eyes laterally. Examination shows complete paralysis of all extremities. Sensation to touch is intact. He undergoes endotracheal intubation for airway protection, and intravenous hydration is begun. An MRI of the brain shows extensive infarction of the base of the pons due to basilar artery occlusion. Conservative treatment is recommended. Although he cannot speak, he can communicate clearly by means of a code using eye blinks. The patient clearly communicates to his physicians repeatedly that he wants no life support or artificial hydration, and wants to die because of his poor prognosis for recovery. His wife and two sons agree with this decision but an estranged daughter does not and is threatening legal action. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: A court order must be obtained to determine what life-sustaining measures, if any, may be withdrawn
options.B: An ethics team consultation should be obtained and their decision followed concerning what life support measures, if any, may be withdrawn
options.C: The patient must be maintained on artificial life support until he dies of natural causes, because there is no consensus in his immediate family
options.D: The patient should be made do-not-resuscitate (DNR) and should be extubated, but all other life-sustaining measures, including artificial hydration, should be continued
options.E: The patient should be made DNR and extubated, and fluids should be withdrawn as he wishes

explanation.correct:
Correct Answer: E.

Neurologically impaired patients should not be assumed to lack decisional capacity to make decisions about their care. The medical team should evaluate the decisional capacity of this patient who is making decisions about his end-of-life care. This patient is able to communicate a consistent decision and demonstrate an appreciation of the consequences of his decision, and therefore, he appears to possess decisional capacity to make the decision to withdraw care. The medical team should respect the patient's autonomy and withdraw care. If a patient lacks decisional capacity, the medical team should identify a surrogate decision-maker. Unless the patient has identified a durable power of attorney, the spouse should serve as decision maker (adult children are typically second in line, followed by parents). Further reassuring in this case is that the patient's wife and adult children corroborate his decision. His estranged daughter's wishes may not reflect the patient's wishes, and given demonstrated decisional capacity with support from surrogates, the patient's wishes should be respected.
explanation.incorrect:
Incorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
A court order (Choice A) or ethics consult (Choice B) are unnecessary. This patient possesses decisional capacity to make the decision to withdraw care. Court orders and ethical consults may be considered when a patient lacks decisional capacity, refuses needed care, and lacks surrogate decision makers.
Leaving the patient on artificial life support (Choice C) or continuing some life sustaining measures (Choice D) counters this patient's autonomy. The medical team should respect the decision of this patient with decisional capacity.
explanation.educationalObjective: Educational Objective: Physicians should evaluate all patients' decisional capacity to make decisions about their care. Patients who possess decisional capacity should have the final say in decisions about their care, even if family members disagree with the decision.
